lesson.title: Methods
lesson.summary: Attach functions to types with a receiver.

sections.heading: What is a method
sections.bodyHtml:
A method is a function with a special extra parameter called a receiver. The receiver sits between func and the function name:

type Person struct {
    Name string
}

func (p Person) Greet() string {
    return "Hi, I'm " + p.Name
}

Call a method with dot notation, just like accessing a field:

alice := Person{"Alice"}
msg := alice.Greet()   // "Hi, I'm Alice"

The receiver p Person means this method is attached to Person. Inside the method, p is a copy of the value you called it on.

sections.heading: Value vs pointer receivers
sections.bodyHtml:
A value receiver ((p Person)) works on a copy — changes inside the method don’t affect the original:

func (p Person) Rename(name string) {
    p.Name = name    // only changes the copy
}

A pointer receiver ((p *Person)) works on the original:

func (p *Person) Rename(name string) {
    p.Name = name    // changes the original
}

Use a pointer receiver when you need to mutate the receiver or when the type is large. By convention, if any method on a type uses a pointer receiver, all methods on that type should use pointer receivers.

Go automatically handles conversion — you can call a pointer-receiver method on a value, and Go will take its address for you.

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml:
Open practice/methods/main.go.

Define a Counter struct with an int field Value. Add a method Increment with a pointer receiver that adds 1 to Value. Create a Counter and call Increment three times, printing the value after each call.

questions.prompt: Where does the receiver go in a method declaration?

options.label: After the method name and before the return type

questions.prompt: If you need a method to modify the receiver’s fields, what kind of receiver should you use?
Can you define methods on types that are not structs (e.g. type MyInt int)?

options.label: Yes, on any type you define in the same package
No, methods only work on structs
Yes, on any type including built-ins like int

exercises.title: Counter with a pointer receiver
exercises.instructions: Edit main.go to define Counter, implement Increment with a pointer receiver, and call it three times.
